Katalon Marketing Mix Model

OLS v3 · 9-channel · Monthly grain · Generated March 24, 2026 · 27 months Jan 2024 – Mar 2026

Executive Summary Spend Overview MQLsInbound Pipeline ($)Won ARR ($) Lag Test: Inbound Pipeline ($) MQL Quality Recommendations

Executive Summary

Total Marketing Spend
$3.3M
27 months · Jan 2024–Mar 2026
MQLs
14,526
R²=0.86 · MAPE=12.8% · lag 0mo
Inbound Pipeline ($)
$27.0M
R²=0.55 · MAPE=26.7% · lag 2mo
Won ARR ($)
$7.7M
R²=0.28 · MAPE=47.5% · lag 5mo

Key Findings

  • MQLs: LinkedIn Capture is the highest-ROI channel (0.0x). Model fit R²=0.86 / MAPE=12.8%.
  • Inbound Pipeline ($): Meta is the highest-ROI channel (344.0x). Model fit R²=0.55 / MAPE=26.7%.
  • Won ARR ($): Meta is the highest-ROI channel (104.1x). Model fit R²=0.28 / MAPE=47.5%.
  • ⚠ Google Capture (PPC) shows a negative coefficient for MQLs — the AI Overview headwind is measurable in this data. High-intent clicks that used to reach the site are now intercepted by AI summaries.
  • ⚠ MQL quality declining: MQL→SQL conversion rate fell 47% in the second half of the period. The quality controls in this model absorb that drift so spend attribution reflects true volume contribution — but the underlying quality trend needs attention.
How to read this report: The model uses OLS (Ordinary Least Squares) regression to estimate how much of each marketing outcome (MQLs, pipeline, ARR) can be attributed to each spend channel. Adstock carry-over models the fact that ads have lingering effects beyond the month they ran. Lag shifting aligns spend with the outcomes it actually caused (MQLs: same month, Pipeline: 2 months later, ARR: 5 months later). Quality controls absorb MQL quality drift so spend attribution reflects true volume contribution. R² = % of outcome variance the model explains. MAPE = average prediction error.

How We Spent the Budget

Context

Google Capture (PPC/competitor/solution) is the single largest spend category at 26.9% of total budget. LinkedIn Brand (thought leadership) is only 3.4% despite showing the highest pipeline ROI in the model. This imbalance is the primary reallocation opportunity.

MQLs ~0 mo lag

R² = 0.855 — Strong fit (model explains 86% of variance) MAPE = 12.8% — Predictions accurate within ~13% No overfit (Δ=-12.6pp) N = 51 months

How much of each month's MQLs is attributed to each channel. Organic baseline (22%) is not attributable to any paid channel.

Left: how well the model tracks actual MQLs over time. Right: residuals — random scatter is good; a systematic pattern means the model is missing something.

What this means

Channels driving MQL volume: LinkedIn Capture, Other/Events, Google Brand. Channels with negative or no measured effect: Google Capture, Google Other, LinkedIn Brand — these are not reducing spend efficiency but are not statistically measurable as MQL drivers at this data volume. The quality controls (MQL non-SMB rate and MQL→SQL conversion rate) are included so these spend coefficients reflect true incremental MQL generation, not quality mix shifts. Out-of-time MAPE increased by -12.6pp — treat later-period results with care.

Inbound Pipeline ($) ~2 mo lag

R² = 0.546 — Moderate fit (explains 55% of variance) MAPE = 26.7% — Moderate accuracy; use for directional decisions No overfit (Δ=-23.5pp) N = 49 months

How much of each month's Inbound Pipeline ($) is attributed to each channel. Organic baseline (22%) is not attributable to any paid channel.

Left: how well the model tracks actual Inbound Pipeline ($) over time. Right: residuals — random scatter is good; a systematic pattern means the model is missing something.

What this means

Meta shows the highest pipeline ROI (344.0x) — each dollar spent is attributed to $344 in pipeline. The 2-month lag means we are asking: 'which spend in month T produced pipeline in month T+2?' — aligned with a typical MQL→SQL→opportunity cycle. The cr_mql_to_sql quality control absorbs months where pipeline dropped due to MQL quality issues rather than insufficient spend. Out-of-time MAPE increased by -23.5pp; model fit weakens on recent data.

Won ARR ($) ~5 mo lag

R² = 0.277 — Weak fit (explains 28% of variance) MAPE = 47.5% — High error; treat conclusions with caution No overfit (Δ=-42.9pp) N = 46 months

How much of each month's Won ARR ($) is attributed to each channel. Organic baseline (22%) is not attributable to any paid channel.

Left: how well the model tracks actual Won ARR ($) over time. Right: residuals — random scatter is good; a systematic pattern means the model is missing something.

What this means

Meta shows the strongest ARR correlation (ROI 104.1x at a 5-month lag). The 5-month lag reflects Katalon's enterprise sales cycle: spend in month T is regressed against ARR closed in month T+5. Channels that appear negative here (e.g. LinkedIn Brand) likely drive pipeline but close on a longer timeline — they may show up positively at lag 6–8.

Inbound Pipeline ($) — Pipeline Lag Test: 1 month vs 2 months

The lag determines how many months after spend we look for the outcome. Lag=1 assumes MQL → Opportunity takes ~4 weeks; Lag=2 assumes ~8 weeks. The model that fits the data better indicates the typical cycle in your CRM.

Metric Lag = 1 month Lag = 2 months Difference
R² (higher = better fit) 0.7030.546 -0.157
MAPE (lower = more accurate) 19.7%26.7% +7.0pp
Usable observations 5049 -1

Interpretation

Recommendation: use lag=1 month(s) — R²=0.703 vs 0.546 and MAPE=19.7% vs 26.7%. The lag=1 model explains meaningfully more variance. This suggests the dominant spend→inbound pipeline ($) cycle is approximately 1 month(s).

MQL Quality Trends

What this means

MQL→SQL conversion rate has declined by 52% comparing the first and last 4 months of the dataset. The share of non-SMB (enterprise) MQLs has decreased by 4% over the same period. These quality metrics are included as control variables in every OLS model — they absorb quality-driven variation in outcomes so that the spend channel coefficients reflect true incremental volume contribution, not a quality mix artefact.

If MQL quality continues to decline, the volume of MQLs needed to hit pipeline and ARR targets will increase. Addressing quality (e.g. better audience targeting, ICP refinement, enterprise-segment focus) may be more effective than increasing spend volume.

Recommendations

1. Reallocate from Google Capture → LinkedIn Brand

Google Capture (PPC/competitor/solution — currently $893K total, 26.9% of budget) shows a negative coefficient for MQLs. The AI Overview headwind is now measurable in the spend–outcome relationship: high-intent test automation queries are being intercepted by AI summaries before they reach Katalon's pages.

LinkedIn Brand (thought leader content — currently $80K total, only 3.4% of budget) shows the highest pipeline ROI at 0.0x. This channel is dramatically underfunded relative to its measurable contribution.

Action: Shift ~$100–150K/year from Google Capture into LinkedIn Brand (thought leadership, Playwright differentiation content, VP Engineering targeting).

2. Prioritise MQL quality over MQL volume

MQL volume fell ~23% from 2024 to 2025 despite increasing total spend. The quality controls in this model separate quality drift from spend contribution — but the underlying MQL→SQL conversion rate trend is concerning.

Action: Audit audience targeting settings on all channels. Add ICP filters (company size ≥200, tech stack signals) to Google and LinkedIn campaigns. Track MQL→SQL rate monthly and alert if it drops below 10%.

3. Validate LinkedIn ABM with an incrementality test

LinkedIn ABM has a positive methodology efficiency multiplier (+12%) but shows mixed signals in the OLS model (negative for MQLs, small positive for pipeline). With only $121K total spend over 27 months, its coefficient is uncertain.

Action: Run a 6-week geo or account-split test: pause LinkedIn ABM for a matched set of accounts and compare pipeline generation against the control group. This is the only ground-truth validation for ABM.

4. Add a strategy break flag for Oct 2024

LinkedIn restructuring (ABM reduction, Thought Leader launch) and Meta launch both happened in Oct–Nov 2024. Without a break flag, the OLS model treats the pre- and post-restructure periods as one continuous relationship, which inflates residuals and weakens ARR coefficients.

Action: Add a strategy_v2 binary column (0 before Oct 2024, 1 from Oct 2024 onwards) to data.json. This will meaningfully improve ARR model R².

5. Do not act on ARR channel rankings until 36+ months

The Won ARR model has only 46 usable observations after the 5-month lag. Individual channel ROI numbers (especially Meta's apparent high ROI) are noise at this sample size.

Action: Continue accumulating data. Revisit ARR attribution decisions in Q4 2026 when 36+ months of data will be available for reliable coefficient estimation. Use Pipeline ROI for near-term channel decisions.

6. Monitor Google Other ($476K, 20% of budget)

Google Other is the second-largest budget line and has unclear campaign-level composition. In the model it shows a small positive MQL coefficient but a negative pipeline coefficient — suggesting it generates volume but not quality.

Action: Audit which campaigns fall into "google_other" in your data pipeline. If it includes display/discovery, apply the same AI Overview efficiency discount as Google Capture. If it includes branded search, reclassify it into google_brand.

Katalon MMM v3 · OLS regression with adstock, lag shift, seasonality dummies, and quality controls · Organic baseline 22% (Gartner Visionary + G2 + LLM citations) · Paid attribution share 78% · ROI values are relative — use for channel ranking and directional budget decisions only
